page.content: In a study involving 307 vaccinated individuals (65.5%) and 162 not vaccinated (34.5%), various determinants were analyzed regarding perceptions of the HPV vaccination. Among those who believed that government information about the vaccine was limited, 103 out of 273 (37.3%) were vaccinated, while 141 out of 145 (97.2%) were not, yielding an odds ratio (OR) of 13.74 (95% CI: 3.82–49.46) with a p-value of <0.001. Trust issues regarding government actions in the presence of serious side effects were noted by 13 out of 273 (4.8%) vaccinated versus 81 out of 145 (55.9%) unvaccinated, resulting in an OR of 10.13 (95% CI: 4.06–25.60) and p-value <0.001. Concerns about insufficient knowledge on vaccine effects were expressed by 153 out of 273 (56%) vaccinated and 143 out of 145 (98.6%) unvaccinated, with an OR of 8.34 (95% CI: 1.41–49.50) and p-value of 0.02. Additionally, 69 out of 273 (25.3%) vaccinated individuals were worried about side effects compared to 127 out of 145 (87.6%) unvaccinated, yielding an OR of 4.71 (95% CI: 2.13–10.44) and p-value <0.001. Regarding disbelief in HPV's harmfulness, 34 out of 273 (12.5%) vaccinated versus 38 out of 145 (26.2%) unvaccinated resulted in an OR of 4.19 (95% CI: 1.63–10.82) and p-value of 0.03. Perceptions of government influence by vaccine producers were indicated by 47 out of 273 (17.2%) vaccinated versus 120 out of 145 (82.8%) unvaccinated, resulting in an OR of 3.60 (95% CI: 1.74–7.47) and p-value of 0.001. Lastly, religious conviction was indicated by 133 out of 273 (48.7%) vaccinated and 101 out of 145 (69.7%) unvaccinated, with an OR of 2.17 (95% CI: 1.09–4.40) and p-value of 0.03. In a separate analysis of correlations, the Spearman correlation for HER3 was 0.363 with a p-value of <0.001, while Akt showed a correlation of 0.028 (p-value 0.765) and 0.201 (p-value 0.028). The correlation for p-Akt was 0.192 (p-value 0.036) and 0.359 (p-value <0.001), mTOR showed a correlation of 0.014 (p-value 0.877) and 0.187 (p-value 0.041), and p-mTOR had a correlation of 0.324 (p-value <0.001), 0.362 (p-value <0.001), and 0.334 (p-value <0.001).

DeterminantsVaccinated n = 307 (65.5%)Not vaccinated n = 162 (34.5%)OR (95% CI)p-value
I think theinformation about the vaccine provided by the government was very limited103/273(37.3%)141/145(97.2%)13.74 (3.82–49.46)<0.001
I have no trust that the government would stop the vaccinations if there was evidence of serious side effects13/273(4.8%)81/145(55.9%)10.13 (4.06–25.60)<0.001
We know way too little about the effects of the vaccine153/273(56%)143/145(98.6%)8.34 (1.41–49.50)0.02
I’m very worried about the side effects of the HPV vaccination69/273(25.3%)127/145(87.6%)4.71 (2.13–10.44)<0.001
I don’t believe HPV can be extremely harmful34/273(12.5%)38/145(26.2%)4.19 (1.63–10.82)0.03
The government is strongly influenced by the vaccine producers47/273(17.2%)120/145(82.8%)3.60 (1.74–7.47)0.001
Religious conviction133/273 (48.7%)101/145 (69.7%)2.17 (1.09–4.40)0.03
